pages:
Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 1 - Sudden and Unexpected Death
Sudden death engages the Garda role across multiple dimensions: classification (suspicious / non-suspicious);
Coroner notification; scene management; identification of deceased; family notification; investigation as
classified; Coroner's inquest support. The Coroners (Amendment) Act 2019 modernised the framework but the
Coroners Act 1962 remains foundational.

1.1 The Coroner System
Coroners are independent judicial officers.
Each Coronial district has a Coroner; Dublin has multiple.
Coroner directs post-mortem in cases of sudden / unexpected / unnatural death.
Coroner conducts inquest determining who, when, where, and how the deceased came by their death.
Coroner can compel evidence; sit with jury in certain cases.
Inquest is not for determining civil / criminal liability; verdict cannot apportion blame.

1.2 Categories of Death Requiring Garda / Coroner Engagement
Sudden / unexpected death - no recent medical attendance / certificate.
Violent death - apparent third-party violence, road collision, fall, etc.
Suspected suicide.
Suspected accidental death.
Death in custody (Garda, prison, mental health detention) - mandatory.
Death of person in State care (Tusla, HSE residential).
Death of foreign national.
Death from suspected poisoning / overdose.
Death where identity unknown.
Death from suspected medical misadventure.
Death from industrial / workplace incident.

1.3 First Response - The DIGNIFIED Framework
1.3.1 Initial Assessment
1.3.2 Suspicious Indicators - Trigger Heightened Response
Apparent injuries inconsistent with self-inflicted / accidental cause.
Signs of struggle - disturbed furniture, defensive marks.
Items moved / removed (e.g., murder concealment).
Witness reports of conflict / violence.
Suicide note in suspicious context (e.g., coercive control history).
Suspect-deceased relationship history (DV / coercive control - Volume 4).
Position of body inconsistent with stated cause.
Toxicological indicators (unusual substances).
Drug-overdose deaths with supply-line investigation potential (Volume 5).

1.3.3 If Suspicious - Crime Scene Protocol
Treat as crime scene - secure, cordon, log entry/exit.
Investigation team engagement - District Detective Sergeant, CSI.
Major investigation framework if appropriate (Volume 9 cross-reference).

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Scene preservation for forensic examination.
Pathology - Coroner directs forensic pathology in suspicious cases.

1.3.4 If Non-Suspicious
Continue with sudden death framework - Coroner notification, post-mortem direction, family notification.
Dignified treatment of deceased - covered, removed appropriately.
Scene release after Coroner / family arrangements.

1.4 Family Notification
Death notification is one of the most consequential interactions a Garda member will conduct. The family will
remember it for the rest of their lives. The standard:
In person where possible - never by phone unless circumstances absolutely require.
Trained Garda member - typically Sergeant or Family Liaison Officer.
Two members ideally - one delivers; one supports.
Private location - take the family inside, not on doorstep.
Direct language - 'I have very sad news for you. I am sorry to tell you that [name] has died.' Avoid euphemisms
('passed away', 'gone') unless culturally specific.
Allow time for the family to absorb.
Practical information when ready - what happens next; Coroner; post-mortem; where the deceased is being
taken.
Welfare support - other family contacted; refreshments; not left alone.
Written information left - contact details for follow-up; support service information.
Follow-up arranged - the FLO function (Chapter 4).

1.5 Identification of Deceased
Formal identification typically by family member - in dignified circumstances.
DNA / dental records where identification not visually possible.
Fingerprints where prints on file.
Tattoos / distinguishing features as supporting evidence.
Mass casualty contexts - DVI (Disaster Victim Identification) framework.

1.6 The Post-Mortem
Coroner directs post-mortem in most sudden death cases.
Standard or forensic post-mortem depending on context.
Pathologist's report - cause of death, manner, any other findings.
Garda member typically attends in suspicious cases - continuity, evidence chain.
Toxicology samples taken routinely.

1.7 The Coroner's Inquest
Inquest follows post-mortem and any criminal proceedings.
Determines: who; when; where; how.
Verdict cannot apportion criminal / civil liability.
Verdicts: accidental death, suicide, misadventure, open verdict, unlawful killing, natural causes, narrative
verdict.
Garda evidence - investigation findings, scene observations, witness liaison.
Family entitled to attend and ask questions through legal representation.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

1.8 Drug-Related Sudden Death
Drug overdose deaths engage both the sudden death framework and the drug-investigation framework (Volume
5):
Investigate the substance source - this is itself a criminal investigation.
Scene preservation - drug paraphernalia, packaging, communications.
Witnesses - last persons with deceased.
Phone forensic examination - communications about drug supply.
Source identification can lead to supply prosecution (s.15 / s.15A MDA).
In some jurisdictions, drug-induced homicide / manslaughter framework engages where supply is identified consult specialist Drugs Unit.

1.9 Death in Custody
Death of a person in Garda custody, prison, or mental health detention engages mandatory frameworks:
Garda Síochána Ombudsman Commission (GSOC) - automatic referral and independent investigation.
Coroner's inquest mandatory - typically with jury.
Article 2 ECHR procedural obligation - effective independent investigation required.
Custody record / CCTV preservation.
Notification to family with appropriate dignity.
Sensitivity to the Article 2 framework throughout - the State's account is part of the family's right to truth.

1.10 Specialist Resources
Crime Scene Investigators (CSI) - Divisional / Regional.
Forensic Pathologist - State Pathologist / Deputy.
Forensic Science Ireland - biological / toxicological / trace analysis.
Garda Technical Bureau - photography, mapping.
National Bureau of Criminal Investigation (NBCI) - serious crime including murder.
Family Liaison Officers.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 2 - Missing Persons
Missing persons investigation engages a distinctive operational framework. Every report is taken seriously; risk
is assessed; response is calibrated to the assessed risk. The Garda National Missing Persons Bureau (within
DSCB) coordinates serious cases; local Districts handle the operational frontline. The investigation may resolve
within hours or extend across decades.

2.1 The Definition - Missing Person
A missing person is anyone whose whereabouts are unknown to those who would reasonably be expected to
know them, where there is concern for their welfare. The definition is broad - it captures:
Adults whose absence is unexplained or concerning.
Children of any age.
Persons with cognitive / mental health vulnerability.
Elderly persons with dementia / confusion.
Persons fleeing DV / abuse contexts.
Persons in custody / care who have absconded.
Persons identified as at risk of self-harm / suicide.
There is no waiting period before a missing person report can be made. The 24-hour myth is operationally false
- immediate reporting and immediate response is the framework.

2.2 The Initial Report
2.2.1 Listen to the Reporter
Account of when last seen / heard from.
Circumstances of disappearance.
Description - clothing, possessions, current state.
Recent history - mental health, conflicts, statements made.
Places of significance - work, school, family, friends, routine destinations.
Contact attempts already made.
Reasons for current concern.

2.2.2 Identification Information
Photograph (recent, clear).
Physical description - height, build, distinctive features, tattoos.
Vehicle (where applicable) - registration, make, model, colour.
Phone number(s).
Social media profiles.
Bank / card details for activity check (where authorised).

2.3 Risk Categorisation
Risk assessment drives the operational response. The framework typically applies four categories:
Category

Description

Operational Response

High

Immediate concern for life;
vulnerability present; risk of harm

Immediate full deployment; DSCB Missing Persons
Bureau notification; specialist resources; public appeal
consideration

Medium

Concern for welfare without
immediate life threat; some

Sustained investigation; routine searches of likely
locations; family / friends contacted

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Category

Description

Operational Response

vulnerability
Low
No
risk

apparent

No specific risk; routine missing
report

Standard investigation; PULSE log; periodic check-back

Adult who has chosen to leave; no
welfare concern

Investigation that the person is safe; respect for privacy if
they wish not to be located

2.3.1 High-Risk Indicators
Child under 13 missing in any circumstance.
Child under 17 missing in concerning circumstances.
Person with dementia / cognitive impairment.
Person with significant mental health crisis - active suicidality.
Person fleeing DV / coercive control context.
Disappearance in suspicious circumstances - signs of struggle, abandoned vehicle, items left.
Person with significant medication needs unable to access.
Vulnerability + adverse weather / exposure risk.
Person of advanced age missing in cold / hostile conditions.
Person on Sex Offender Register breaching notification.

2.4 Operational Response - High-Risk
Garda National Missing Persons Bureau (DSCB) notification.
Resource deployment - local search teams; Air Support Unit where available; search dogs.
Public appeal consideration - photograph and description released.
AMBER Alert in qualifying child abduction cases (Chapter 2.6).
CCTV recovery from likely route.
Communications data access where appropriate (under statutory framework).
Phone location services - where lawful (Garda Cyber Crime Bureau support).
Bank / card activity check - where appropriate authorisation.
Hospital checks - the missing person may be unidentified in A&E.
Mortuary checks - unidentified deceased.
Other agencies - mental health services, addiction services, prison service, immigration.

2.5 Investigation Standard - Sustained Cases
Designated Investigator (Sergeant level typical).
Investigation log - all steps documented contemporaneously.
Family liaison - sustained engagement.
Regular review of progress.
PULSE updates.
Coordination with other Districts if person may have travelled.
International coordination via INTERPOL where relevant.
Long-term missing - periodic review; specialist sustained engagement; cold-case approach if extended.

2.6 The AMBER Alert Framework
AMBER Alert is the rapid public alert system for child abductions where the child is believed to be in serious
danger. Operational criteria (typical):
Child under 18 abducted.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Belief that the child is in immediate danger of serious harm or death.
Sufficient descriptive information to make alert useful.
Garda HQ activation following Senior Investigating Officer recommendation.
AMBER Alert engages multiple platforms - SMS to phones in area, motorway signs, broadcast media, social
media. False or misjudged alerts undermine the system - rigorous criteria application.

2.7 Child Missing - Specific Framework
Children First Act 2015 framework - Tusla notification.
Specific protocols for under-13s.
School / care setting absconding.
Children in State care - specific Tusla / Garda protocols.
Repeat missing children - sustained engagement vs case-fatigue risk.
Trafficking / sexual exploitation considerations - DSCB specialist engagement.

2.8 Unidentified Persons and Long-Term Missing
Found deceased without identification - the framework operates in reverse.
National database of missing persons cross-referenced.
Forensic identification - DNA, dental, fingerprint.
Distinguishing features.
Reconstruction techniques.
Public appeal for identification.
Long-term missing - cases reviewed periodically; technology developments (familial DNA, etc.) may resolve
historical cases.

2.9 The Resolved Outcome
Person located safe - welfare check, return / pickup arranged, support services where appropriate.
Person located deceased - notification protocol, sudden death framework (Chapter 1).
Person located in custody / hospital / care - confirmation with reporter; arrangements for engagement.
Person located but does not wish to return - adult right respected (subject to safeguarding analysis if
vulnerability).
Person not located despite full investigation - long-term framework engages; family liaison continues; case
open until resolved.

2.10 The Family Liaison Dimension
Missing persons families experience unique distress - the absence of resolution is itself an injury. The family
liaison function (Chapter 4) is operationally central. Standards:
Designated liaison contact - typically District-level.
Regular updates - even where there is nothing to report; the family must not feel forgotten.
Honesty - including about limits of investigation and unknowns.
Support service referral - Missing Persons charity (Missing in Ireland Support Service), bereavement supports
if applicable, counselling.
Long-term commitment - the case persists; the engagement persists.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 3 - Mental Health - s.12 Mental Health Act 2001
The Garda has a specific welfare power under s.12 Mental Health Act 2001 to remove a person from a public
place to a mental health facility where the person is believed to be suffering from a mental disorder and there
is serious likelihood of harm. The framework is fundamentally welfare-oriented, not punitive. This chapter sets
out the operational application.

3.1 The Statutory Framework
s.12 MHA 2001

3.1.1 The Two Limbs
Reasonable grounds for believing the person is suffering from a mental disorder.
Reasonable grounds for believing that, because of the mental disorder, there is a serious likelihood of immediate
and serious harm to self or others.

3.1.2 'Mental Disorder' - MHA 2001 Definition
Mental illness, severe dementia or significant intellectual disability where one of the following applies:
Serious likelihood of immediate and serious harm to the person or to others, OR
The judgment is so impaired that failure to admit would be likely to lead to serious deterioration of the condition
/ would prevent the administration of appropriate treatment that could be given only by such admission.
Operational note: s.12 engages the first limb (serious likelihood of harm). The judgment-impairment limb
operates through the medical / civil framework.

3.2 The Operational Sequence
3.2.1 Initial Encounter
Call typically arises from family / member of public / hospital / self-presenting / observation.
Approach calmly - reduce stimulation, lower voice, allow space.
Assess - is the person in mental health crisis; is there immediate risk; can the person be safely engaged.
Communication - patient, non-confrontational, validating without colluding with delusion.

3.2.2 Forming Reasonable Grounds
Observation - speech, presentation, content of expressions, behaviour.
Account from family / others - history, recent change, expressed intent.
Statements by the person - direct expressions of intent to harm self / others.
Visible indicators - weapons, ligature, self-injury, height-risk position.
Specific factors that engage the 'serious likelihood of immediate harm' threshold.

3.2.3 De-escalation - The Preferred First Step
Where de-escalation is possible and the person can be safely supported, that is the preferred route. Options:
Family / trusted person attendance.
Local GP attendance (where available and willing).
HSE Mental Health Service contact - mental health Crisis Resolution Team (CRT) where operational in area.
Voluntary attendance at hospital / approved centre.
Voluntary engagement with mental health services.

3.2.4 s.12 Removal - Where Criteria Met

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

3.3 The Approved Centre Framework
Mental Health Act 2001 framework operates.
Admission under s.13 / s.14 / s.15 framework (involuntary admission for assessment / treatment / by application
of family / by Garda removal under s.12 leading to formal admission).
Independent medical assessment required.
Patient rights framework - tribunal review, legal representation, access to inspector.
Garda role ends with handover to approved centre.

3.4 Custody-Stage Mental Health Concerns
A person in Garda custody may present with mental health crisis - either pre-existing or arising during detention.
Framework:
Member in Charge assesses on book-in.
Medical examination where indicated.
Mental health professional engagement where indicated.
Suicide / self-harm prevention - enhanced observation, ligature removal, supervision.
Where person in custody appears to meet s.12 criteria and the underlying criminal matter can be deferred,
removal to approved centre may be appropriate.
Death in custody - any death engages Article 2 framework (Chapter 1).

3.5 Suicide Intervention
Specific operational training for talking with suicidal persons.
Active listening; validation; reduction of agitation.
Removal of means where possible.
Engagement of family / trusted person.
Critical Incident Response (CIR) framework for high-risk situations - specialist Garda engagement.
Subsequent welfare engagement after immediate crisis.

3.6 The Welfare Framework Around Mental Health
HSE Mental Health Services - statutory provider.
Pieta House - suicide and self-harm support.
Samaritans - emotional support helpline.
Console (legacy) / now operating frameworks.
HSE National Self-Harm and Suicide Prevention Strategy.
Local Community Mental Health Teams.
GPs as primary care entry point.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 4 - Family Liaison
The Family Liaison Officer (FLO) function is a specific Garda role: providing sustained, sensitive engagement
with families affected by sudden death, missing persons, serious incidents, and other special situations. The
FLO is part investigator, part communicator, part welfare contact - operating at the intersection of the
investigation and the family's needs.

4.1 The FLO Role
Single named contact between family and investigation.
Trained Garda member - typical training course on family liaison.
Sustained engagement - through investigation, prosecution (if any), Coroner's inquest, beyond.
Information conduit - providing what can be provided; explaining what cannot yet be provided.
Welfare support - linking family to appropriate services.
Cultural / religious sensitivity - the family's specific traditions and practices respected.

4.2 When FLO is Engaged
Sudden / unexpected death - typically.
Murder / manslaughter investigation - mandatory.
Fatal road traffic collision.
Missing person - long-running cases especially.
Death of foreign national - additional embassy / consular engagement.
High-profile / sensitive incidents.

4.3 The Operational Framework
4.3.1 Initial Engagement
Death notification (where applicable) - per protocol at Chapter 1.4.
Introduction of FLO role.
Contact details provided.
Initial information shared - what is known, what is being investigated, what happens next.
Family questions answered honestly - including 'we don't know yet'.

4.3.2 Sustained Engagement
Regular contact - even where there is nothing new to report.
Updates on investigation progress (subject to operational considerations).
Preparation for milestones - post-mortem, inquest, court process.
Accompaniment to formal events (inquest, court hearing) where requested.
Welfare check-in - the family's coping over time.

4.3.3 Information Management
What can be shared - the investigation's general status, the protective framework around the family, public
information.
What cannot be shared - operational specifics that would compromise investigation, suspect details before
charge, third-party private information.
Honesty about limits - 'I can't share that yet, but I will tell you when I can.'
Consistency - the family should hear the same information from FLO as from public sources.
Documenting communications - what was said, when, to whom.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

4.4 Cultural and Religious Considerations
Practices vary - body release timing; funeral arrangements; mourning customs.
Cultural advisers where helpful.
Religious leader engagement at family request.
Language - interpreters where required.
Immigrant families - additional dimensions (consular engagement, family abroad, immigration status
considerations).

4.5 The Long Engagement
Some FLO engagements last years. Cases progress to trial; appeals; inquest; sometimes never resolve. The FLO
function operates throughout. Specific considerations:
Continuity - the same FLO where possible; transition managed where rotation necessary.
File - the family liaison record continues alongside the investigation file.
Periodic review - the family liaison plan revisited.
Welfare for the FLO - sustained engagement carries cost; supervisor and welfare service engagement.

4.6 The Limits of FLO
FLO is not a therapist - signposting to mental health / bereavement support where required.
FLO is not the investigator - separation of roles preserves integrity.
FLO does not advocate against the State / process - but is a conduit and a person of trust.
FLO must not promise what cannot be delivered - integrity of trust is the operational asset.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Chapter 5 - Other Special Incidents
This chapter consolidates briefer operational guidance on incidents that engage the special-incidents
framework: bomb threats / suspicious devices; hostage / barricade; major emergencies; large-scale public order
incidents; missing children specifically; suspected mass-casualty contexts. Each engages specialist resources
and command frameworks.

5.1 Bomb Threats / Suspicious Devices
Specialist - Garda / Army EOD (Explosive Ordnance Disposal) for confirmed or suspected devices.
First response - cordon, evacuate, control.
Distance / safety zone calibrated to potential device size.
Communication restriction - mobile phones / radios may trigger certain devices; specific protocols apply.
Specialist arrival - EOD takes scene command.
Hoax investigations - charge framework (criminal damage, false report).

5.2 Hostage / Barricade Situations
Specialist - Garda Critical Incident Response (CIR) / negotiators.
First response - contain, communicate, do not assault.
Command structure - Critical Incident Commander appointed.
Negotiator engagement at earliest opportunity.
Tactical team for entry / resolution if required.
Patience - protracted negotiations are routine; rushed resolution increases risk.
Family / victim support throughout.

5.3 Major Emergencies
Major Emergency Management framework - multi-agency.
Gardai, Fire Services, Health (NAS / HSE), Local Authority.
Command structures - National Emergency Coordination, regional, on-scene Gold / Silver / Bronze.
Specific frameworks for: severe weather, flooding, transport incidents, public health, public order.

5.4 Public Order at Scale
Volume 3 cross-reference.
Public Order Unit deployment.
Command structure for sustained operations.
Mass arrest protocols.
Documentation under sustained pressure.

5.5 Mass Casualty / Disaster Victim Identification
DVI framework - Garda Technical Bureau / international cooperation.
Identification protocols - dental, DNA, fingerprint, distinguishing features.
Family liaison at scale.
Coordination with hospitals, Coroners, religious authorities.

5.6 Garda Welfare in Special Incidents
Cumulative exposure cost is real and operationally significant.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Critical Incident Stress Debriefing where applicable.
Peer support frameworks.
Employee Assistance Programme.
Periodic welfare engagement - not just post-major-incident but ongoing.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Appendix - Practice, Charge Selection, File Craft (Special
Incidents)
Part A - Defences and Issues in Special Incidents Cases
Special incidents typically engage non-criminal frameworks (Coroner, Mental Health Tribunal, civil) rather
than defences in the criminal sense. Where criminal investigation arises from special incidents (sudden death
progressing to homicide; missing persons resolving to suspect identification; mental health crisis with criminal
conduct), the relevant defences operate per Volume 2 / specific volume.

Part B - Death-Related Charge Selection
Conduct

Charge

Unlawful killing with intent / s.4 1964 reckless harm
intent

Murder

Unlawful killing without murder mens rea

Manslaughter

Drug supply causing death

s.15 MDA + potentially manslaughter (case-specific)

Negligent conduct causing death (non-driving)

Manslaughter (gross negligence) - rare

Failure of care duty to vulnerable person

Children First / Tusla framework; specific neglect
offences

Causing death by dangerous driving

s.53 RTA 1961 (Vol 7)

Aiding and abetting suicide

Criminal Law (Suicide) Act 1993

Concealment / disposal of body

Specific offences; obstruction of inquest

Part C - File Craft for Special Incidents
C.1 Sudden Death File
C.2 Missing Persons File
C.3 s.12 MHA File
C.4 FLO Engagement Record

Evidence

and

Part A - Scene Evidence in Sudden Death
Scene photographed before disturbance.
Items in situ; mapping for major incidents.
Forensic preservation where suspicion.
Chain of custody for any items seized.
CSI engagement.

Part B - Post-Mortem and Toxicology
Coroner-directed post-mortem - foundational evidence.
Toxicology - SATU / FSI standard.
Pathologist statement - cause of death; manner; other findings.
Standard or forensic pathology depending on classification.

Part C - Missing Persons Evidence
Reporter statement.
Last contact witnesses.
CCTV from likely routes.
Communications data (statutory framework).
Phone location services (legal authorisation).
Forensic evidence at point of disappearance (where applicable).

Part D - Mental Health Encounter Documentation
Notebook entry capturing observations supporting reasonable grounds.
Time and place of removal.
Force used (if any) and justification.
Approved centre handover documentation.
Where criminal conduct was present, separate criminal file.

Part E - Death in Custody Evidence
Custody record - complete and contemporaneous.
CCTV from custody area.
Member-in-Charge observations log.
Medical assessment records.
Welfare check timestamps.
Cell condition documentation.
Personal effects accounting.
Notifications log (family, GSOC, supervisors).

Part F - Family Liaison Communications
Contemporaneous record of every contact with family.
What was said; what was undertaken.

(Special

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Family questions and responses.
Documentation supports investigation file and ultimate Article 2 compliance review.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Appendix - Multi-Agency Working in Special Incidents
Part A - Coroner Services
Coronial districts.
Dublin Coroner; other regional Coroners.
Coroner's office - case management, post-mortem direction, inquest scheduling.
Garda liaison via designated Coroner liaison officer.

Part B - HSE Mental Health Services
Community Mental Health Teams (CMHT).
Crisis Resolution Teams (where operational).
Approved Centres for involuntary admission.
National Office for Suicide Prevention.

Part C - Hospital and Medical Services
Hospital A&E for casualty intake.
State Pathologist for forensic post-mortem in suspicious cases.
GP services for community-level mental health support.

Part D - Tusla
Child welfare interface in missing children, child death.
Children First mandatory reporting framework.
Social work alongside Garda investigation.

Part E - Garda Specialist Resources
Garda National Missing Persons Bureau (DSCB).
National Bureau of Criminal Investigation.
Garda Technical Bureau.
Crime Scene Investigators.
Family Liaison Officers.
Critical Incident Response.

Part F - Support Services for Bereaved / Vulnerable
Missing in Ireland Support Service.
Console / Pieta House / Samaritans - mental health crisis.
Bereavement support services - various NGOs.
Victim Services Office (Department of Justice).

Part G - GSOC
Garda Síochána Ombudsman Commission.
Mandatory referral for deaths and serious harm involving Gardai.
Independent investigation engages Article 2 procedural requirement.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Appendix - Statutory Anchors (Vol 8 - Special Incidents)
Precise statutory wording for principal special-incidents provisions.

Part A - Coroners Framework
Coroners Act 1962 - foundational; appointments, jurisdiction, powers.
Coroners (Amendment) Act 2019 - modernised framework; expanded scope; family rights; jury inquest
provisions.
Inquest categories; verdicts available; family rights to representation and examination.

Part B - Mental Health Act 2001
s.3 - definition of 'mental disorder'.
s.12 - Garda power to remove person in public place.
s.13-15 - involuntary admission framework.
Mental Health Tribunal review provisions.
Mental Health Commission framework.

Part C - Children First Act 2015
Mandatory Reporting; Mandated Persons.
Tusla referral framework.
Garda Children First Officer at District level.

Part D - Death-Related Criminal Provisions
Murder / manslaughter (Volume 2).
Concealment of body - common law / specific offences.
Criminal Law (Suicide) Act 1993 - aiding / abetting suicide.

Part E - Garda Síochána Acts
Garda Síochána Act 2005 - GSOC framework; mandatory referral provisions.

Part F - Civil Defence / Major Emergencies
Major Emergency Management framework - multi-agency.
Civil Defence Act 2002.

Part G - Privacy / Communications Data
Communications (Retention of Data) Acts 2011, 2022.
Statutory authorisation framework for access to retained data in serious cases including missing persons.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Appendix - Case Law Resource (Vol 8 - Special Incidents)
Special incidents engage a distinctive ECtHR jurisprudence on the State's procedural obligations in respect of
death (the Article 2 procedural obligation - McCann, Jordan, Da Silva, Salman). Mental health detention
engages Article 5 / 8 ECHR. Sudden death and missing persons engage the State's positive protective
obligations (carrying from Vol 4 / Vol 6 Opuz / O'Keeffe lines). This appendix treats the principal authorities
in vLex-style depth.
Source verification reminder
Article 2 procedural-obligation jurisprudence continues to develop. Mental Health Act 2001 case law (review
tribunal decisions, judicial review of detentions) develops continuously. Verify all citations on vLex Ireland,
BAILII, HUDOC (ECtHR) before relying.

Part A - Article 2 ECHR Procedural Obligation on State Investigation of
Death
A.1 McCann and Others v UK (1995) 21 EHRR 97 - Foundational
Citation: Application No. 18984/91, Grand Chamber judgment 27 September 1995.
Facts: Three IRA suspects were shot dead by British SAS soldiers in Gibraltar. The applicants (the families)
argued that the killings violated Article 2 ECHR and that the subsequent investigation (inquest) was inadequate.
The Grand Chamber considered both the substantive killings and the procedural obligation.
Holding: Violation of Article 2. The substantive use of lethal force was disproportionate (the Court found it
was avoidable in the circumstances). The case also established the procedural obligation - State has duty to
conduct an effective investigation where individuals are killed by State agents.
Reasoning: Article 2 requires States to take appropriate measures to safeguard life. The duty extends to
ensuring an effective investigation where lives are lost - particularly where the State or its agents are involved.
The investigation must be: (a) effective in establishing the facts; (b) independent of those implicated in the
events; (c) prompt and reasonably expeditious; (d) involve sufficient public scrutiny; (e) involve the next of kin
to the extent necessary to safeguard their legitimate interests.
Significance: Landmark ECtHR authority on Article 2. Establishes the procedural obligation that informs all
State investigation of death - particularly deaths in custody, deaths involving State agents, and unexplained
suspicious deaths.
Operational Application: The Garda investigation of any death engages the McCann procedural framework
where State actors are involved or the death is suspicious. Operational consequences: (a) independence of
investigation - investigators not involved in events under review; (b) effectiveness - thorough investigation of
all relevant circumstances; (c) promptness - investigation begins immediately; (d) family involvement - to the
extent compatible with the investigation; (e) ultimate scrutiny - Coroner's inquest, GSOC (where relevant),
criminal proceedings. The Garda role supports the McCann framework through effective initial response and
investigation.

A.2 Jordan v UK (2001) 37 EHRR 2
Citation: Application No. 24746/94, judgment 4 May 2001.
Facts: Pearse Jordan was shot dead by RUC officers in Northern Ireland. The applicant (his father) argued the
investigation had been inadequate. The Court considered the procedural obligation in detail.
Holding: Violation of Article 2 procedural limb. The investigation had failed across multiple dimensions independence (the RUC investigating themselves); promptness (delays in inquest); scope (limited public
scrutiny); family involvement (inadequate).
Reasoning: The Court elaborated the McCann framework. The procedural obligation requires specific
operational features. Failures across multiple features compound.
Significance: Refines and develops the McCann procedural framework. The 'Jordan factors' are now the
operational template for assessing investigation adequacy under Article 2.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Operational Application: Investigation of any death involving Garda action or in custody engages the Jordan
template. GSOC framework operates to provide independence in respect of GSOC-referred matters. Coroner's
inquest provides ultimate scrutiny. Garda investigation is the operational foundation that supports these
subsequent processes.

A.3 Da Silva v UK (2016) Application No. 5878/08
Citation: Application No. 5878/08, Grand Chamber judgment 30 March 2016.
Facts: Jean Charles de Menezes was shot dead by Metropolitan Police on London Underground in 2005,
mistaken for a suspected bomber. The applicant (a cousin) challenged the procedural adequacy of investigations
and the prosecution decision.
Holding: No violation of Article 2 procedural limb. The investigation and review framework met the Article 2
standard - extensive IPCC investigation, criminal review, civil proceedings, inquest.
Reasoning: The Grand Chamber confirmed the McCann / Jordan framework. Where the investigation
framework as a whole - across criminal review, civil process, inquest, regulatory review - meets the procedural
standards, Article 2 is satisfied even where individual elements have limitations.
Significance: Confirms the cumulative-framework approach. The State's compliance is assessed across the full
investigation / review apparatus, not each element in isolation.
Operational Application: The Garda investigation is one element in the cumulative framework. Robust Garda
investigation supports the overall Article 2 compliance. GSOC, Coroner, criminal proceedings, civil process the full framework operates.

A.4 Salman v Turkey (2000) 34 EHRR 425
Citation: Application No. 21986/93, Grand Chamber judgment 27 June 2000.
Facts: Salman died in custody in Turkey. The Turkish authorities asserted natural causes; the family argued he
was killed in custody. The case established that where a person dies in State custody, the burden of explanation
falls on the State.
Holding: Violation of Article 2 - substantive and procedural. The State could not provide a plausible account
of how Salman died; the investigation had been inadequate. Where a person enters custody healthy and emerges
deceased, the State must account for what happened.
Reasoning: Persons in custody are under the State's control. The State has an enhanced duty of explanation
and of effective investigation. The burden shifts to the State where unexplained deaths occur in custody.
Significance: Foundational on State responsibility for custody deaths. The 'Salman duty of explanation'
operates wherever persons die in State custody.
Operational Application: Garda custody deaths engage the Salman framework rigorously. Custody record,
CCTV, medical assessment, observation logs - all must support the State's account. GSOC referral is
mandatory. The Garda investigation supports the Article 2 obligation through evidential rigour.

A.5 Osman v UK (1998) 29 EHRR 245 - Positive Obligation
Operational Application: (Treated in detail in Vol 4 caselaw.) Applied to special incidents - where Gardai are
on notice of risk to identified individual from third-party violence, operational steps to protect engage. The
McCann procedural obligation and the Osman protective obligation operate together - both before (protect) and
after (investigate) harm to life.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part B - Irish Authority on Coroner Framework
B.1 The Coroners Act 1962 / Coroners (Amendment) Act 2019
The Coroner system is foundational. Key Irish authorities relate to: scope of inquest, verdicts available, family
rights, evidence at inquest, judicial review of Coroner decisions. The 2019 Act modernised the framework expanded scope, enhanced family rights, jury inquest provisions.

B.2 Magee v Farrell - Coroner Decisions Reviewable
Coroner decisions on scope of inquest / verdicts available are reviewable by judicial review. Significance: the
inquest framework is supervised by the High Court for compliance with statutory scope and fairness.

B.3 Family Rights at Inquest
Modern Irish practice recognises substantial family rights at inquest - legal representation, witness examination,
narrative verdicts where appropriate, sustained engagement. Recent decisions emphasise the family-protective
dimension of inquest.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part C - Mental Health Detention Authority
C.1 Article 5 ECHR - Detention of Persons of Unsound Mind
Article 5(1)(e) ECHR permits the detention of 'persons of unsound mind' subject to specific safeguards. The
Winterwerp v Netherlands (1979) framework establishes:
Unsoundness of mind established by objective medical expertise.
Of a kind / degree warranting compulsory confinement.
Validity of continued confinement depends on persistence of disorder.
Periodic review by independent body.

C.2 M.S. v Ireland - Mental Health Tribunal Authority
Various Irish authorities on the Mental Health Act 2001 framework - tribunal review, patient rights, scope of
admission powers, judicial review. The framework has been substantially supported by judicial decisions while
also being subject to ongoing reform.

C.3 Bournewood Principle - DOL
The HL v UK (Bournewood) principle - persons whose freedom is restricted in practice require the safeguards
of formal detention framework even where not formally 'detained' in name. Operational implication: Garda
removal under s.12 MHA engages the formal framework safeguards - the person is in custody / under State
control and Article 5 attaches.

C.4 The s.12 MHA Operational Framework
Specific Irish authorities on s.12 MHA emphasise:
Reasonable grounds required - articulable basis.
Welfare framework not punitive.
Force only as necessary.
Handover to medical assessment promptly.
Documentation supports judicial review if applicable.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part D - Missing Persons and Vulnerable Adults
D.1 Osman Positive Obligation - Missing Persons
Where Gardai are on notice of imminent risk to an identified vulnerable person who is now missing, the Osman
positive obligation may engage. Operational implication: high-risk missing persons cases involving vulnerable
adults engage the Article 2 protective framework - the response must be calibrated to the risk.

D.2 Child Missing - Children First / Tusla Interface
Missing child cases engage the child protection framework alongside the missing persons response. O'Keeffe
v Ireland (Vol 6 case law) establishes the State's positive obligations to children - missing children
investigations operate within this protective framework.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part E - Procedural Authorities Applied
E.1 Volume 2 Procedural Framework
Walsh / Madden / Buck / Gormley / Doyle / JC / Damache / Casey - all carry to special incidents context with
operational adaptation. Particular relevance:
Walsh - reasonable suspicion for arrest in special incidents (e.g., suspect identified at sudden death scene).
Madden - voluntariness particularly relevant in mental-health-context interviews / statements.
JC - procedural rigour in special incidents investigations.
Damache - search warrants in special incidents (e.g., suspect premises in sudden death).

E.2 Casey - Identification in Missing Persons
Where missing person is identified through visual identification (e.g., body found, family identification), Casey
framework operates.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Part F - Key Operational Takeaways from Case Law
F.1 The McCann Procedural Framework
Effective; independent; prompt; sufficient public scrutiny; family involvement. The five Jordan factors. The
Garda investigation supports the cumulative State compliance.

F.2 The Salman Custody Death Burden
Where person dies in State custody, the State must account. Garda custody records, CCTV, medical assessment
- all support the State's account.

F.3 The Osman Protective Obligation
Operates pre-emptively in special incidents - protective response to identified risk in DV, mental health crisis,
missing person vulnerability, threats to known individuals.

F.4 The s.12 MHA Welfare Framework
Welfare not punitive. Force proportionate. Handover to medical promptly. Article 5 safeguards engage.

F.5 The Coroner Framework
Inquest is supervised by High Court for fairness and statutory scope. Family rights substantial. Garda evidence
is foundational.

F.6 Family Liaison
Effective family liaison supports the McCann 'family involvement' procedural requirement. Honesty,
consistency, sustained engagement.

F.7 Procedural Rigour Across the Long Timeline
Special incidents cases extend over months / years. Procedural compliance across the timeline is the operational
standard. Documentation contemporaneous; chain of custody preserved; investigation log complete.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

How to Use Volume 9
Volume 9 consolidates the cross-cutting operational frameworks that operate across Volumes 1-8. The chapters
cover topics that engage multiple offence categories rather than belonging to any one:

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

The Cross-Cutting Operational Framework
Volumes 1-8 covered substantive offence categories and incident types. Volume 9 covers what runs through
them all - the powers, tools, structures, and frameworks that operate regardless of which Volume's offence is
under investigation. The volume is designed for the reference moment - when the Garda member needs the
specific framework on which the current investigation rests, irrespective of offence type.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Volume 9 Core Mnemonics
ARMS - Firearms / Weapon Investigation
A - Authority - what statutory power authorises Garda action.
R - Registration - is the firearm registered; certificate status.
M - Movement - chain of custody; weapon examination; ballistics.
S - Suspect linkage - who possessed; who fired; intent.

DATA - Communications Data Request
D - Define what data is sought; specificity to the investigation.
A - Authorise per statutory framework.
T - Transmit request to service provider with appropriate authority.
A - Analyse and integrate with investigation.

CUSTODY - Detention Framework Application
C - Caution administered on arrest.
U - Understand the applicable detention regime (CJA 1984 / DTA 1996 / OAS Act).
S - Stages of extension - authority at each level.
T - Time recording at every stage.
O - Observation log throughout.
D - Disclosure of rights to detained person.
Y - Yield to medical / legal / welfare needs.

COMMAND - Incident Command Framework
C - Command structure established (Gold / Silver / Bronze).
O - Objectives defined.
M - Methods and resources allocated.
M - Multi-agency coordinated where applicable.
A - Action plan implemented.
N - Notification and updates.
D - Documentation contemporaneous.
Verification reminder
Communications data law has been substantially reshaped by CJEU jurisprudence (Tele2/Watson 2016; La
Quadrature 2020; Privacy International). Irish framework has been amended (Communications (Retention of
Data) Act 2022). Verify current statutory text and case law before relying. Firearms law is subject to periodic
review and amendment. Specialist resources are subject to organisational change at Garda HQ level.

Garda Investigation Techniques - Complete Reference

Garda Code of Ethics - Cross-Cutting Application
The cross-cutting frameworks engage the Code of Ethics in distinctive ways. Use of weapons; access to
communications data; deployment of specialist resources; detention powers; disclosure obligations - each has
its own ethics dimensions.

Standard 1 - Duty
The duty to protect life shapes the use of every cross-cutting tool. Communications data accessed to locate a
missing person; firearms enforcement to prevent serious harm; specialist resources deployed in proportionate
response to assessed risk.

Standard 4 - Use of Force
Weapons use is the apex of the use-of-force framework. Authorised Firearms Officers (AFOs) and the
Emergency Response Unit operate within stringent training, authorisation, and accountability frameworks. The
threshold for use of any firearm by a Garda is high; the threshold for use of lethal force is higher still.

Standard 5 - Honesty and Integrity
Communications data; PULSE / PEMS records; custody records; disclosure - each is foundational. The
contemporaneous record is the legal and operational truth; honesty in entry is the standard.

Standard 6 - Impartiality
Detention; specialist deployment; weapons engagement - all engage at the most consequential end of Garda
action. Impartiality across demographics, neighbourhoods, communities is the standard.

Standard 7 - Speaking Up
Where cross-cutting frameworks are misapplied - communications data accessed without proper authority;
detention extended without proper justification; weapons engaged without proper threshold - the duty to speak
up applies.

The Five Cross-Cutting Trip-Wires
